rationale: The citing paper uses Thompson and Norton (2011) as direct theoretical support for its purchase-timing argument, specifically the claim that prepurchase thinking emphasizes having a product more than using it. That borrowed idea is used to motivate H4 (attenuation of the variety effect in prepurchase evaluation) and is revisited when interpreting Study 5 results. This is more than a passing literature-list mention because it informs hypothesis development and mechanism interpretation.
relation_description: Etkin and Sela apply Thompson and Norton's having-versus-using insight to a new context: how perceived variety of usage experiences affects product evaluation. They use this prior idea to predict and explain why their effect weakens in prepurchase (vs. postpurchase) judgments.
